title: Streaming Responses
summary: Stream tokens as they are generated to drastically cut perceived latency and keep the user engaged.
problem: LLMs can take several seconds to generate a full response. Waiting 5 seconds for a block of text feels broken and causes users to abandon the task.
forces: Users expect immediate feedback, ideally under 400ms.
Reading takes time, so if text appears as it's generated, the user reads while the model computes.
solution: Use Server-Sent Events (SSE) or WebSockets to stream tokens to the UI as soon as the model predicts them. The time-to-first-token (TTFT) drops to milliseconds, making the system feel instant.
tradeoffs: Increases UI complexity (handling partial markdown, auto-scrolling).
May require intermediate states if the AI must think or use tools before streaming text.
body:
## First principles
Human perception of time is non-linear. A 3-second wait with a spinner feels like an eternity. A 3-second generation where text streams in feels like a fast typist. **Streaming turns wait time into reading time.**

## Why it feels faster
By minimizing Time-To-First-Token (TTFT), you provide immediate visual feedback. The user starts consuming the information while the rest is being generated. This masks the total latency of the request and keeps the user's attention anchored on the content.

## Implementation notes
Streaming requires parsing partial content—like unclosed markdown tags or code blocks—gracefully. UI must auto-scroll intelligently, pausing if the user scrolls up to read.
analogy: It's like a fast-talking friend who starts answering your question immediately while they're still figuring out the end of the sentence, rather than sitting in silence for a minute before delivering a prepared speech.
diagram:
sequenceDiagram
  participant U as User
  participant S as Server
  U->>S: Request
  S-->>U: Token 1 (200ms)
  U->>U: Starts reading
  S-->>U: Token 2
  S-->>U: Token N

examples.what: Provides built-in React hooks (useChat) that handle token streaming and partial state automatically.
Streams text character-by-character, drastically reducing perceived latency.

drills.q: What metric is most improved by streaming?
Why does streaming improve perceived latency?
drills.a: It allows the user to start reading and processing information while the model is still computing the rest.

teachItBack: Instead of making the user wait 5 seconds for a complete answer, you stream the text token by token. The first word appears in milliseconds, and the user reads along as the AI generates. It transforms a frustrating wait into an engaging, dynamic experience.

glossary.definition: Time To First Token; the duration from the user's request to the appearance of the very first character of the AI's response.

quiz.q: What is the primary UX benefit of token streaming?
choices: It cuts perceived latency by providing immediate feedback
It ensures the answer is more accurate
quiz.explanation: Streaming gives the illusion of speed by giving the user something to read immediately, masking the total generation time.
